## [00:00 - 03:01]

In this lesson, I'm going to cover how to optimize your influencer funnel to drive sales. An influencer funnel illustrates the customer journey from brand discovery through an influencer to purchase and all of the way through to post-purchase. If you don't create an influencer funnel, all of the brand buzz that your influencers will generate will hit a dead end. There wouldn't be an intentional specific goal to lead your customers to, and you could be missing out on almost all possible sales. If you don't optimize your funnel, one stage or one piece of the puzzle can throw off the entire experience and keep your customers from purchasing. So an influencer funnel has four stages. The first is brand awareness, which is when an influencer posts on your brand. The next stage is the consideration stage. Here it's all about getting people to follow your brand on social. This will result from including specific call to actions on influencer posts. After the consideration stage is the conversion stage. Once you get to the middle stage, you want people to pull out their credit cards and actually buy from you. So it's really important to get their email address here. This is the most important stage, as email marketing is still one of the lead drivers of my sales. In fact, 40% of my e-commerce store sales now come through email alone. So it's important to then have a really strong offer to sign up to email. With the fifth, our offer was extremely strong because we only sold in the fifth of each month for five days. So our first offer was sign up to find out when we're launching again, really triggering people's FOMO. Last is brand loyalty. These are the repeat buyers, the result of a successful customer journey. To optimize for brand loyalty, you want your influencers to constantly be posting about your brand to create top of mind positioning. Also, now that your customers following you on social, you're able to constantly expose them to your brand message, which will create a higher customer lifetime value and more repeat buyers. As you can tell, the influencer funnel is a series of specific decisions on a brand's behalf for their social channels. Now we're going in on the most important part of the funnel, which is profile optimization. Profile optimization is honestly one of the biggest make or break elements that I've seen when it comes to the success or failure of influencer marketing campaigns. And I see this happen time and time again with brands. You may have done absolutely everything right in your influencer campaign leading up to this point, from campaign planning to creation, to identifying and finding the right influencers to collaborate with, reaching out, building a relationship and briefing them on your campaign. However, when they post, if this one thing isn't right, your entire campaign could collapse beneath it. While on YouTube, an influencer is able to hyperlink into the description of their post. That is not the case on Instagram.

## [03:01 - 06:04]

So in this case, the only way that they can actively link to your brand is by adding your handle. So for example, at DropBottle. That means rather than clicking through to your conversion rate optimized landing page like on YouTube, on Instagram, they'll be tapping through to your brand's Instagram profile. So it makes sense here that your Instagram account acts like the initial landing page for your influencer marketing activities. And that's why it's so important to have your Instagram account optimized for conversion. Just like in any conversion targeted activity, you want to make the process as simple, enticing and streamlined as possible. When optimizing your social media profile to convert, the three main things that you want to focus on are your feed, your bio and the link in your bio. So in terms of your feed, your content should have a similar look, feel and aesthetic to the influencers that you're working with in your campaign. This is why selecting influencers that are on brand from a content perspective is important. This way, they'll instantly know that your account's similar to an account that they already know and love. Therefore, they'll be more likely to follow you or follow through and perform another action like visiting your website or even making a purchase on the spot. Moving on to optimizing the bio of your social profile now. A new follower should be able to quickly breathe through your bio and understand exactly what your brand is all about. So your bio should act like an elevator pitch for your brand. It should cover who you are or what you're selling, how your product is unique or different so your unique value proposition and a call to action. So first up, you want to write a brand summary and your brand's main value proposition. Think of it like this. What makes your product unique that would entice a customer to buy and why should they trust you? Now the challenge is to say that in as few words as possible. One of my favorite communications quotes is whoever says that the simplest wins. For example, Skinny Me Teas Instagram Bio currently reads, So that's what the product is, our UVP, some social proof and a call to action or CTA. In terms of your CTA, this should incentivize your followers to perform a certain action usually tapping the link in your bio. So let's talk about some examples of call to actions that you can have in your bio. So some examples could be sign up to our wait list via the link in bio if you're in pre-launch. In this case, exclusivity and newness are the incentives to sign up. You could also use scarcity as an incentive through emphasizing that supplies are limited. For example, only 100 units left in stock or 72 hours left in our spring clearance sale. Don't miss out. Another way it could be get access to X piece of free content. For example, our 14 day booty challenge via the link in our bio, then using an email unlock platform like Just Do Know or Sumo Me to grow your email list.

## [06:04 - 09:05]

This one resource alone for Skinny Me Tea got us over 8,000 new email sign ups within 24 hours of it going live by pushing it across my Instagram accounts to convert our followers into our email subscribers. This is often called a lead magnet and other ideas for SMT could include a five day eating plan or five foods that reduce bloating, any content that your audience would find valuable. So in this case, content is the incentive to sign up. Another example could be the use of a discount code. For example, Insta 25, the $25 off your first purchase to drive sales, or if you don't want to offer discount codes, you can add a link to a shoppable version of your social feed on your website. There are a lot of apps that allow you to easily create and manage these. For example, our code Pinterest 10 that we include in a lot of our Pinterest posts and in our bio on Pinterest has driven over 5,000 purchases and over $294,000 in sales for us. So in this case, clearly saving money is the incentive to purchase here. Another example could be follow us to win X product to drive followers. And of course, in this case, the giveaway is the incentive to follow. Or we could be reinforcing the CTA of the influencer campaign itself. Whatever that may have been, for example, follow us to win a beauty prize pack valued at $2,500. The call to action will depend on your goals and it's good when starting out to try some different ones for a week or so to see what converts best. I'd further emphasize these call to actions in your posts themselves. For example, when our drop bottle was recently out of stock, in every single post would push our followers to sign up to our waitlist to be notified when the bottles are back in stock. With this method, and by changing the entire user experience of our website to be geared toward lead generation, we were able to grow a waitlist of over 50,000 people waiting to get their hands on our new and improved bottle. So the link in your bio could include links to a waitlist landing page, links to unlock free content by entering their email, links to your best sellers, or links to a shoppable feed. The list goes on. I think the link work harder for you by making it a trackable short link with Google UTM parameters so that you can track the number of people who click it, see how they convert and retarget to them. One last idea could be to do a blog post featuring a big macro influencer before their content goes live so that when it does, their followers visit your brand profile, they'll see a relevant reason to click through to your website right away. A smart thing to do here could be to organize with the influencer so that you can use some of the images from their shoot for your brand throughout the blog post and make sure that the blog post contains shoppable links where relevant. As an action item for this lesson, go ahead and map out your four stage influencer funnel using the diagram provided in the workbook. Then I'd like you to begin to optimize your feed, bio, and the link in your bio to maximize the conversions of your influencer campaigns.

## [09:05 - 09:26]

First you want to make sure that your feed aesthetic suits the kinds of influencers that you're working with. Then you want to make sure that your bio is optimized by making it clear what you sell, your UVP, some social proof and a call to action. And finally, you want to make sure that the link in your bio is working as hard for you as possible. So go ahead, do that now and I'll see you in the next lesson.
